en.extract:
The rufous-bellied tit (Melaniparus rufiventris) is a species of bird in the tit family. It is found in Africa from the Republic of the Congo, Democratic Republic of the Congo and northern Namibia east to Tanzania and northern  Mozambique.


Taxonomy
The rufous-bellied tit was formally described in 1877 by the Portuguese zoologist José Vicente Barbosa du Bocage based on a specimen collected near the town of Caconda in central Angola. He placed it with the tits in the genus Parus and coined the binomial name Parus rufiventris. The specific epithet is Modern Latin meaning "red-bellied", from Latin rufus meaning "ruddy" or "rufous" and venter, ventris meaning "belly". The rufous-bellied tit is now one of 14 tits placed in the genus Melaniparus that was introduced in 1850 by the French naturalist Charles Lucien Bonaparte.
Five subspecies are recognised:

M. r. rufiventris (Barboza du Bocage, 1877) – south Congo and south DR Congo to central Angola and central Zambia
M. r. masukuensis (Shelley, 1900) – southeast DR Congo, east Zambia and Malawi
M. r. diligens (Clancey, 1979) – south Angola, north Namibia, southwest Zambia and northwest Botswana
M. r. pallidiventris (Reichenow, 1885) – Tanzania, south Malawi and north Mozambique
M. r. stenotopicus (Clancey, 1989) – east Zimbabwe and central west Mozambique
The subspecies M. r. pallidiventris and M. r. stenotopicus have sometimes been considered as a separate species, the cinnamon-breasted tit. It was thought that iris color was a reliable feature to differentiate the two species but a study published in 2015 found that in western Tanzania birds with dark eyes and pale eyes occurred in the same flocks. This suggests that it might be better to consider the cinnamon-breasted tit as a subspecies of the rufous-bellied tit.


Description
This 15 cm (5.9 in) long bird has a black head, breast, wings and tail, grey upperparts, white fringes to the wing feathers, and rufous underparts. The adult has a yellow eye, brown in the duller juvenile.
Its habitat is subtropical or tropical dry miombo forests.


References

Harrap and Quinn, Tits, Nuthatches and Treecreepers Christopher Helm, 1996 ISBN 0-7136-3964-4


External links

Rufous-bellied Tit - Species text in The Atlas of Southern African Birds.
Image at ADW

The '''rufous-bellied tit''' ('''''Melaniparus rufiventris''''') is a species of [[bird]] in the [[tit (bird)|tit]] family. It is found in [[Africa]] from the [[Republic of the Congo]], [[Democratic Republic of the Congo]] and northern [[Namibia]] east to [[Tanzania]] and northern  [[Mozambique]].

==Taxonomy==
The rufous-bellied tit was [[species description|formally described]] in 1877 by the Portuguese zoologist [[José Vicente Barbosa du Bocage]] based on a specimen collected near the town of [[Caconda]] in central Angola. He placed it with the tits in the [[genus]] ''[[Parus]]'' and coined the [[binomial name]] ''Parus rufiventris''.<ref>{{ cite journal | last=Barbosa du Bocage | first=José Vicente | author-link=José Vicente Barbosa du Bocage | date=1877 | title=Mélange ornithologique: II Espèces nouvelles d'Angola | journal=Jornal de Sciencias Mathematicas, Physicas e Naturaes | language=French | volume=6 | pages=158-162 [161] | url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/39190277 }}</ref><ref>{{ cite book | editor-last=Paynter | editor-first=Raymond A. Jr | year=1986 | title=Check-list of Birds of the World | volume=12 | publisher=Museum of Comparative Zoology | place=Cambridge, Massachusetts | page=103 | url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/14482220 }}</ref> The specific epithet is [[Modern Latin]] meaning "red-bellied", from [[Latin]] ''rufus'' meaning "ruddy" or "rufous" and ''venter'', ''ventris'' meaning "belly".<ref>{{ cite web | last=Jobling | first=James A. | title=rufiventris | work=The Key to Scientific Names | url=https://birdsoftheworld.org/bow/key-to-scientific-names/search?q=rufiventris | publisher=Cornell Lab of Ornithology | access-date=9 March 2025 }}</ref> The rufous-bellied tit is now one of 14 tits placed in the [[genus]] ''[[Melaniparus]]'' that was introduced in 1850 by the French naturalist [[Charles Lucien Bonaparte]].<ref name=ioc>{{cite web| editor1-last=Gill | editor1-first=Frank | editor1-link=Frank Gill (ornithologist) | editor2-last=Donsker | editor2-first=David | editor3-last=Rasmussen | editor3-first=Pamela | editor3-link=Pamela C. Rasmussen | date=February 2025 | title=Waxwings and their allies, tits & penduline tits  | work=IOC World Bird List Version 15.1 | url=http://www.worldbirdnames.org/bow/waxwings/ | publisher=International Ornithologists' Union | access-date=9 March 2025 }}</ref>

Five [[subspecies]] are recognised:<ref name=ioc/>
* ''M. r. rufiventris'' ([[José Vicente Barbosa du Bocage|Barboza du Bocage]], 1877) – south Congo and south DR Congo to central Angola and central Zambia
* ''M. r. masukuensis'' ([[George Ernest Shelley|Shelley]], 1900) – southeast DR Congo, east Zambia and Malawi
* ''M. r. diligens'' ([[Phillip Clancey|Clancey]], 1979) – south Angola, north Namibia, southwest Zambia and northwest Botswana
* ''M. r. pallidiventris'' ([[Anton Reichenow|Reichenow]], 1885) – Tanzania, south Malawi and north Mozambique
* ''M. r. stenotopicus'' (Clancey, 1989) – east Zimbabwe and central west Mozambique

The subspecies ''M. r. pallidiventris'' and ''M. r. stenotopicus'' have sometimes been considered as a separate species, the cinnamon-breasted tit. It was thought that iris color was a reliable feature to differentiate the two species but a study published in 2015 found that in western Tanzania birds with dark eyes and pale eyes occurred in the same flocks. This suggests that it might be better to consider the cinnamon-breasted tit as a subspecies of the rufous-bellied tit.<ref name=ioc/><ref>{{ cite journal | last=Anderson | first=Jason | year=2015 | title=Eye colour variation in Rufous-bellied Tit ''Melaniparus rufiventris'' in western Tanzania | journal=Bulletin of the British Ornithologists' Club | volume=135 | issue=2 | pages=98–107 | url=https://boc-online.org/bulletins/downloads/BBOC1352-Anderson.pdf }}</ref>

==Description==
This {{cvt|15|cm}} long bird has a black head, breast, wings and tail, grey upperparts, white fringes to the wing feathers, and rufous underparts. The adult has a yellow eye, brown in the duller juvenile.

Its [[habitat]] is subtropical or tropical dry [[miombo]] [[forest]]s.
